questionText: Prove that the product of any two consecutive even integers is divisible by $8$.

steps.description: Represent the first even integer algebraically.
steps.explanation: Every even integer is $2$ times an integer, so writing the first one as $2n$ captures all even numbers without loss of generality.

steps.description: Write the next consecutive even integer.
steps.explanation: Consecutive *even* integers differ by $2$, not $1$, so the next even number after $2n$ is $2n+2$.
The claim is about the product, so we multiply the two even integers together.

steps.description: Factor a 2 out of the second bracket.
steps.explanation: Both factors are even, so each contributes a factor of $2$. Pulling these out gives an explicit $4$, which is already two-thirds of the way to $8$.

steps.workingLatex: \text{need one more factor of } 2 \text{ from } n(n+1)
steps.explanation: We have $4n(n+1)$; to reach a multiple of $8$ we must show $n(n+1)$ contributes one further factor of $2$, i.e. that it is even.

steps.workingLatex: n \text{ and } n+1 \text{ are consecutive integers}
steps.explanation: $n$ and $n+1$ follow one after another, and among any two consecutive integers exactly one is even. We confirm this with a short two-case check.
If $n$ itself is even, the product $n(n+1)$ clearly contains a factor of $2$.
If $n$ is odd then $n+1$ is even, so again the product picks up a factor of $2$. These two cases cover every integer $n$.
In both cases $n(n+1)$ is even, so it equals $2k$ for some integer $k$.
Replacing $n(n+1)$ with $2k$ turns the factor $4$ into $8$, giving the multiple of $8$ we were aiming for.
Since $P = 8k$ with $k$ an integer, the product is divisible by $8$ for every choice of consecutive even integers.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Writing the integers as $2n$ and $2n+2$, the product is $4n(n+1)$. As $n(n+1)$ is a product of consecutive integers it is even, say $2k$, so $P=8k$. Hence the product is divisible by $8$. $\blacksquare$
